narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've poured into your undergrad work and LSAT prep—it's tough to hear that your path to law school hit this roadblock based on our prediction model. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a story: the model saw your Year 3 decile of 3.0 as the opening chapter that most strongly signaled a risk of struggling on the bar exam, suggesting those later grades placed you in the lower third of your peers when momentum matters most. Right behind that, your LSAT score of 30 stood out as a pivotal hurdle, pulling the prediction firmly toward failure since it falls short of the benchmarks that typically light the way to success. Your Year 1 decile of 4.0 added to that narrative, showing an early pattern that didn't build the strong foundation the model looks for in bar passers. Even with your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.7, your middle family income bracket whispered concerns about resources that often support the grind ahead, nudging the story further toward risk. Being a white female pursuing full-time studies wove in subtle threads from patterns in similar profiles, each tilting the scales a bit more toward that 46% chance of not passing the bar. It's not that any one piece defined you—your strong UGPA tried to counterbalance things—but together, they painted a picture of someone the model worries might face steeper odds, much like others who've walked this path before. This isn't a judgment on your potential or drive; it's just the model's way of spotting patterns to protect spots for those it sees as safer bets. If you boost that LSAT or strengthen those later deciles in future pursuits, the story could shift dramatically. You've got resilience in you—keep writing your own ending. With empathy, Your Admissions Advisor
